source.title: State of U.S. Tariffs: SCOTUS Ruling Update (Yale Budget Lab)

extractions.claim: SCOTUS IEEPA ruling cut effective tariff rate on Chinese imports by almost two-thirds, but de minimis exemption status remains unresolved
extractions.evidence: On Feb 20, 2026, SCOTUS held in Learning Resources v. Trump that IEEPA does not authorize unilateral tariffs (6-3 decision, Roberts writing). IEEPA invalidation cut effective rate on Chinese imports by almost two-thirds. Average effective tariff rate fell from 16.0% to 9.1% (still highest since 1947). However, SCOTUS was silent on whether ruling applied to de minimis exemption — the longstanding rule allowing packages <$800 duty-free. Trump had suspended de minimis in summer 2025, and not much changed after ruling for cheap items ordered from abroad.

verification.verifier_notes: Yale Budget Lab primary research, SCOTUS opinion 24-1287. Marketplace.org confirmed de minimis ambiguity.

extractions.claim: Post-SCOTUS, Trump imposed replacement 10% Section 122 tariffs that expire after 150 days (by July 24, 2026)
extractions.evidence: After SCOTUS ruling, Trump imposed 10% tariff under Section 122 of Trade Act of 1974 (and suggested raising to 15%). Critical constraint: Section 122 tariffs expire automatically after 150 days — by July 24, 2026 — unless Congress votes to extend. Section 301 tariffs on China remain active. Tax Policy Center estimated replacing IEEPA with Section 122 reduces tariff costs on apparel/leather from 15% to 8% relative to production costs.

verification.verifier_notes: Tax Policy Center, Yale Budget Lab, SCOTUSblog all confirm Section 122 replacement and 150-day expiry.

extractions.claim: Temu's US pricing in March 2026: cheaper than domestic alternatives even with remaining tariffs, but de minimis not restored
extractions.evidence: Despite SCOTUS ruling, 'not much changed after the ruling for cheap items ordered from abroad' per Marketplace.org. De minimis exemption question unresolved. Consumers continued ordering from Temu/Shein because 'even with tariffs, it's often cheaper than shopping domestically.' Remaining tariffs imply consumer price increase of 0.6% (~$800/household). Post-substitution: 0.5%, $600 loss per household.

verification.verifier_notes: Marketplace.org (Mar 3, 2026) and Yale Budget Lab. Key finding: SCOTUS helped but didn't fully restore pre-tariff Temu economics.
